Summary of what was found: Amcor positions AmPrima for home and personal care as a recycle-ready mono-material flexible-packaging platform that can run on commercial converting and filling equipment at the same speeds as non-recyclable materials and can deliver up to 90% packaging-weight reduction versus rigid refill formats.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-material recycle-ready flexible pouches for home and personal care, aimed at refill use cases. Packaging format relevance: refill pouches for rigid bottles/containers. Weight/material reduction evidence: up to 90% packaging weight reduction. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: directly relevant to shampoo, body wash, hand wash, and similar liquid personal-care categories where the rigid hero pack can remain and the refill does the lightweighting. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market/high-volume refill systems; technically possible as a consumer-facing refill but not inherently suited to replacing a premium hero pack on shelf. Performance/recyclability/filling-line limitation notes: Amcor ties the route to commercial-speed converting/filling and recycle-ready design rather than aesthetic luxury cues. Use in screening: treat mono-material refill pouches as a core shortlist for mass personal care when the product can be decanted into a reusable rigid bottle and local flexible-pack recycling pathways exist.
Source name: AmPrima Recycle-Ready Packaging for home and personal care — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/sustainability/products/amprima/emea/homeandpersonalcare

Summary of what was found: Mondi’s mono-material stand-up pouch for liquids is a PE-based duplex structure offered for liquid soap and detergent refills; the HFFS version can include aroma barrier and weight-loss avoidance, has a wide sealing window, and can be specified with glossy or matte finishing.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: PE-based mono-material refill pouch. Packaging format relevance: stand-up refill pouch, pre-made or HFFS, sized from 0.5 L to 3 L. Weight/material reduction relevance: no numeric weight-saving figure, but supports mono-PE as a lighter refill route versus traditional rigid refill bottles by showing a purpose-built flexible replacement for liquid formats. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: highly relevant to shampoo, conditioner, body wash, hand wash, and similar liquid beauty/personal-care refills. Premium versus mass-market fit: primarily mass-market; glossy/matte finish option shows flexible mono-material refill packs can support elevated shelf appearance where needed. Performance/barrier/filling limitation notes: aroma barrier, weight-loss avoidance, and a wide sealing window indicate mono-material PE can be engineered for fragranced liquid products and production robustness. Use in screening: classify PE mono-material refill pouches as a high-priority route for liquid personal care, especially where fragrance retention and filling-line seal robustness are critical.

Summary of what was found: Mondi and Henkel launched a recyclable mono-PE refill pouch for Pril that reduced plastic use by 70%, worked with a reusable rPET pump bottle, and was described as leak-proof and durable enough for home deliveries.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-PE refill pouch used in a reuse-plus-refill system. Packaging format relevance: refill pouch feeding a durable rigid dispenser bottle. Weight/material reduction evidence: 70% plastic reduction versus the prior format/system as described by the partners. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: dishwashing liquid example is transferable to liquid personal care because dispensing logic, refill behavior, leak risk, and home-delivery stress are similar to shampoo, body wash, and hand soap refills. Premium versus mass-market fit: best for mass-market and mainstream refill systems; less relevant as a standalone premium primary package. Performance/barrier/e-commerce limitation notes: the pouch is described as leak-proof and highly durable for home deliveries, valuable for e-commerce and transport screening. Use in screening: justify mono-PE refill pouches as realistic and scaled when paired with a reusable rigid dispenser and validated for transport integrity.
Source name: Mondi & Henkel partner to launch fully recyclable mono-material refill pouch for Pril — Mondi Group — https://www.mondigroup.com/news-and-insight/2022/mondi--henkel-partner-to-launch-fully-recyclable-mono-material-refill-pouch-for-pril/

Summary of what was found: Mondi’s Berlingot refill format is a mono-material PE solution that reduces plastic usage by more than 75% compared with traditional rigid plastic bottles.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: ultra-light mono-PE refill pack. Packaging format relevance: compact refill/reserve pack style flexible format. Weight/material reduction evidence: over 75% plastic reduction versus rigid bottles. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: relevant to concentrated liquid top-ups, reserve refills, travel refills, or applications where extreme material reduction matters more than shelf rigidity. Premium versus mass-market fit: much stronger for mass-market and cost-sensitive refill systems than premium/luxe hero packs. Performance/consumer-handling limitation notes: strong on material reduction, but does not claim suitability for highly sensitive formulas or prestige presentation. Use in screening: treat very-light refill sachet/pouch formats as a high-impact route for products that do not require high-rigidity packaging at point of use.

Summary of what was found: L’Oréal states that Elvive refillable pouches reduce plastic use by 60% across several haircare lines, while the same page shows luxury refills are being scaled in fragrance and skincare rather than only mass-market haircare.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: refill systems in beauty, including flexible pouches in consumer haircare and refill systems in luxury fragrance/skincare. Packaging format relevance: refill pouches for haircare and refill solutions across fragrance, skincare, hygiene, and makeup. Weight/material reduction evidence: Elvive refillable pouches reduce plastic by 60%; Prada Paradoxe refilling saves 44% glass, 67% plastic, 100% metals, and 61% cardboard versus repurchase of the full pack. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: direct beauty-sector evidence from a major global player. Premium versus mass-market fit: supports two-track logic: flexible refill pouches are strong in mass-market, while premium/luxe can retain the premium outer shell and move material reduction into the refill system. Performance/brand limitation notes: source frames refills as compatible with desirability and performance, but does not imply that a standalone flexible pouch replaces the premium shelf pack. Use in screening: support separate mass-market and premium refill tracks, strongest where the consumer keeps a durable outer pack.
Source name: Pioneering the refill revolution — L’Oréal Groupe — https://www.loreal.com/en/articles/commitments/pioneering-the-refill-revolution/

Summary of what was found: Kenvue reports that Le Petit Marseillais shower-product doypack refills cut plastic by at least 50%, removed more than 80 metric tons of virgin plastic annually, and sold more than 1.5 million units in Europe since 2025.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: flexible doypack refill pouches for personal care. Packaging format relevance: shower-gel refill pouches, later expanded into larger hand-wash refill formats. Weight/material reduction evidence: at least 50% less plastic than a standard bottle; more than 80 metric tons of virgin plastic removed annually. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: direct current evidence from a personal-care brand showing commercial-scale adoption rather than a pilot concept. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong mass-market route, particularly for rinse-off liquid categories. Performance/consumer limitation notes: strong on scale and material reduction but does not provide high-barrier or premium-perception claims; use as evidence for operationally practical mainstream refills. Use in screening: place doypack refill pouches in the mass-market shortlist for shower, hair, and hand-care categories where consumer reuse of the original bottle is feasible.
Source name: How Le Petit Marseillais reduced plastic packaging by 50% with doypack refills — Kenvue — https://www.kenvue.com/our-commitments/how-le-petit-marseillais-reduced-plastic-packaging-by-50-with-doypack-refills

Summary of what was found: Glenroy says personal-care flexible packaging must be selected around formulation aggressiveness and fragrance retention: active ingredients, acids, and other compounds can require chemically resistant, high-barrier structures, and product, shelf-life, distribution, and equipment conditions all influence film choice.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: flexible films and laminations for personal care/cosmetics. Packaging format relevance: pouches, flow wraps, sachets, and other flexible formats for personal-care products. Weight/material reduction relevance: no weight number; explains why not every light flexible structure is acceptable for cosmetics because barrier and chemical resistance can dominate film selection. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: directly relevant to shampoos, sunscreen, blemish treatments, anti-aging creams, toothpaste, and flavored gels. Premium versus mass-market fit: applies to both; premium/luxe may need stronger sensory, fragrance, and appearance retention, while mass-market needs scalable robust films. Performance/barrier/compatibility limitation notes: flags aggressive formulations and fragrance/flavor retention as reasons common films may fail; also points to product/package compatibility testing and barrier optimization. Use in screening: filter out lightweight pouch routes that cannot handle actives, acids, fragrance retention, or required shelf life.

Summary of what was found: Glenroy’s pouch and sachet page shows flexible sachets and related laminations are used for shampoo, conditioner, sunscreen, lotion, serums, and cosmetic samples, and successful structures depend on barrier, puncture resistance, seal compatibility, and compatibility testing.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: pouch, sachet, and related film laminations, including recyclable and PCR options. Packaging format relevance: sachets, pouches, sample packs, and stick-pack-adjacent formats for personal care and cosmetics. Weight/material reduction evidence: sachets use less packaging per unit than rigid containers and can lower material cost while improving portability and sampling efficiency. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: directly relevant for samples, travel sizes, low-price sachets, and small dose packs. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market, travel, and sample distribution; weak as a premium hero-pack replacement. Performance/barrier/filling-line limitation notes: oxygen/moisture/light barriers, chemical resistance, puncture and tear resistance, heat-seal compatibility, and formulation compatibility testing are highlighted. Use in screening: shortlist sachets and small pouches for samples/trials/low-cost dose delivery, but treat cautiously where high-barrier multilayers or aggressive formulations are required.

Summary of what was found: APR’s PE flexible-packaging guide sets practical design-for-recycling rules: EVOH can be used in PE structures within defined limits, PE closures are preferred, rigid or non-PE attachments need testing, metal can make the pack non-recyclable, and inks/adhesives must be minimized or tested.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-PE and PE-rich flexible packaging design rules for recycling. Packaging format relevance: bags, pouches, sachets, wraps, and flexible PE packaging with closures, spouts, labels, inks, and adhesives. Weight/material reduction relevance: not a weight-saving source; shows constraints required so lightweight PE films remain viable in future recycling systems. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: directly relevant to mono-PE refill pouches and reserve packs for liquid personal care and cosmetics. Premium versus mass-market fit: highly relevant to mass-market scaling because decoration-heavy packs, rigid attachments, and metalized elements can undermine recyclability; also relevant to premium packs that often over-decorate. Performance/recyclability limitation notes: APR prefers EVOH within defined limits and PE closures; rigid/non-PE attachments need testing; metal is non-recyclable in this stream; inks/adhesives can cause gels and specks. Use in screening: apply as a hard filter for mono-PE pouch design, especially for barrier content, fitments, inks, adhesives, and decorative elements.
Source name: PE Flexible — Association of Plastic Recyclers — https://plasticsrecycling.org/apr-design-hub/apr-design-guide/pe-flexible/

Summary of what was found: APR’s PP flexible guidance shows mono-PP routes are possible but sensitive to barrier levels and decoration: EVOH within defined limits is acceptable, while metalized layers, paper labels, nitrocellulose/untested inks, heavy adhesives, and certain coatings can impair recyclability or require testing.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-PP and PP-rich flexible packaging recycling design criteria. Packaging format relevance: PP flexible films and pouches, including labels, inks, adhesives, closures, and metalized decoration. Weight/material reduction relevance: not a quantitative weight source; explains what a commercially realistic recyclable mono-PP route can and cannot include. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: important for wet wipes, some flow-wraps, and certain pouch formats where PP is the chosen single-polymer route. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong for mass-market wet-wipe and hygiene-style formats; caution for premium packs relying on metalized decoration, paper labels, or heavy wet-chemistry coatings. Performance/recyclability limitation notes: EVOH ≤6 wt% with PP-g-MAH tie layers is acceptable; metalized content and paper labels can be detrimental; printing, adhesives, and curable coatings can create gels/specks and degrade recyclate quality. Use in screening: apply as the main design-for-recycling screen for mono-PP beauty/wipe packs, especially when PCR or PPWR-readiness is considered.
Source name: PP Flexible — Association of Plastic Recyclers — https://plasticsrecycling.org/apr-design-hub/apr-design-guide/pp-flexible-guidance/

Summary of what was found: RecyClass reports that low levels of PP contamination in PE film can already hurt recycled-film quality: at 2.5% PP concentration in the test sample, tear resistance and elongation dropped while gels/specks increased; EVOH above 5% of PE-film weight also harms PE-film recyclability.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: recyclability interaction of PE films with PP, PA, and EVOH. Packaging format relevance: PE-targeted mono-material pouches that nevertheless contain PP layers/components or barrier layers. Weight/material reduction relevance: not a lightweighting claim; recyclability risk source because many light flexible packs are near-mono rather than truly mono-material. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: highly relevant to refill pouches with PP fitments, PP labels, or excessive barrier content. Premium versus mass-market fit: crucial for both; especially mass-market where recyclability claims must survive at scale and premium-decorated packs may add incompatible materials. Performance/recyclability limitation notes: quantified recyclate-quality deterioration from PP contamination and confirms EVOH above 5% harms PE recyclability. Use in screening: keep PP content and EVOH content tightly controlled in PE-targeted refill routes or the lightweight pouch may become a poor recycling design.
Source name: Recyclability of Flexible Polyethylene with PP & PA: Novel Findings — RecyClass — https://recyclass.eu/news/recyclability-of-flexible-polyethylene-with-pp--pa-novel-findings/

Summary of what was found: CEFLEX’s barrier-packaging guidance says pouches with fitments or spouts require case-specific testing to prove they can be sorted into the flexible-packaging stream, and structures outside the recommended polyolefin combinations need further testing to qualify for recycling.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: design-for-recyclability rules for plastic-based flexible barrier packaging. Packaging format relevance: barrier pouches, including spouted or fitment-bearing structures. Weight/material reduction relevance: not a weight claim; system-level recyclability constraint for lightweight barrier packs. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: highly relevant because many liquid beauty/personal-care refill pouches need spouts, zippers, sliders, or higher barriers. Premium versus mass-market fit: applies to both; especially important in premium packs where extra components and decorative layers are more common. Performance/recyclability limitation notes: spouts/fitments need specific testing; non-PE/non-PP combinations and out-of-window constructions need additional evidence; guidance is cautious rather than permissive. Use in screening: treat recyclability claims for spouted beauty refills as conditional until the whole structure, not just the film, is validated.
Source name: Pioneer Project Barrier Summary Table — CEFLEX — https://ceflex.eu/public_downloads/Worksheet_Project_Barrier_final_Nov_2020_a.pdf

Summary of what was found: Menshen explains that mono-material spouted pouches are hard to produce on legacy HFFS equipment because conventional spouts and machines were built for mixed-material laminates; its LoTUS spout was developed to improve sealing, avoid low-melting additives, avoid speed loss, and even raised line speed by 5 pouches per minute.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-material spout/fitment engineering for spouted pouches. Packaging format relevance: spouted pouches on HFFS equipment. Weight/material reduction relevance: indirect; lightweight mono-material pouches can fail operationally if the fitment system is not redesigned. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: very relevant to shampoo, liquid soap, lotion, and refill pouches that depend on spouts for clean pouring and leakage control. Premium versus mass-market fit: more important for mass-market scaling because line speed and machine conversion cost matter heavily; premium use possible at lower volumes. Performance/filling-line limitation notes: sealing quality, machine speed, and spout/laminate heat behavior are core constraints. Use in screening: classify spouted mono-material pouches as a separate, higher-validation-risk route than simple non-spouted reserve pouches.
Source name: Game Changer in Production of Sustainable Mono-material Spouted Pouches — MENSHEN — https://menshen.com/innovation-en/game-changer-in-production-of-sustainable-mono-material-spouted-pouches/

Summary of what was found: Gualapack lists cosmetics and beauty among key spouted-pouch markets and notes that pouches may use PE- or PP-based, alu-free, alu-based, mono-material, mixed-polyolefin, or bio-based structures depending on application; recyclability depends on material combination and local recycling streams, and barrier performance declines once opened.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: pre-made and spouted pouches across multiple material systems. Packaging format relevance: pre-made pouches, reclosable spouted pouches, no-spill formats, and low-viscosity liquid applications. Weight/material reduction evidence: spouted pouches typically require less material than rigid containers and reduce transport volume; pre-made spouted pouches significantly reduce material usage versus some form-fill-seal alternatives. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: direct because cosmetics and beauty are listed among served markets. Premium versus mass-market fit: best for functional refill and portable liquid formats; less suitable as full premium hero-pack substitute without a durable outer system. Performance/barrier/handling limitation notes: spout diameter must match viscosity; hot-fill suitability is material-dependent; once opened, original oxygen/moisture barrier is no longer fully intact; recyclability is conditional on structure and geography. Use in screening: frame spouted pouches as versatile but not universal, especially for sensitive formulas or markets without flexible-pack recycling.

Summary of what was found: ExxonMobil highlights two relevant points for lightweight beauty flexibles: a mono-material HFFS pouch case with 60% material reduction versus rigid thermoformed trays, and a non-laminated MDO PE pouch route with enhanced mechanical properties and significant downgauging potential.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: MDO PE and mono-material downgauged flexible films/pouches. Packaging format relevance: HFFS pouches and mono-material PE laminates or non-laminated structures. Weight/material reduction evidence: 60% material reduction for one mono-material pouch case; significant downgauging potential from MDO PE. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: useful for beauty/personal-care refill pouches, reserve packs, and certain sachet formats where better stiffness, toughness, and optics are needed from a PE route. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market because downgauging and operational efficiency are the stated value proposition; can support more premium-looking flexibles if optics and printability are sufficient. Performance/barrier/filling-line limitation notes: focuses on mechanical-property improvement and process efficiency rather than formula barrier needs, so barrier-sensitive beauty products still require validation. Use in screening: shortlist MDO-PE and similar film-engineering routes as enablers of thinner mono-material films where barrier requirements stay manageable.

Summary of what was found: Mondi’s re/loop FlowWrap for wet wipes is a mono-PP, form-fill-seal structure for baby care, cosmetics, and household wipes that contains 35% PCR, is compatible with PP mechanical recycling per CEFLEX guidelines, and showed sealing, barrier, machinability, and line-speed performance comparable to virgin alternatives.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-PP recyclable flexible structure with PCR content. Packaging format relevance: flow-wrap and sachet-like wet-wipe packaging. Weight/material reduction relevance: lightweight flexible replacement for harder-to-recycle laminate structures rather than a rigid-vs-flex comparison; key evidence is that mono-PP route can retain performance while being designed for recycling and incorporating PCR. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: directly relevant to cosmetic wet wipes, beauty wipes, and adjacent hygiene formats. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market and high-volume wipe categories; less directly relevant to prestige skincare or fragrance. Performance/PCR/filling-line limitation notes: internal tests and customer trials showed comparable performance to virgin materials; compatible with existing lines and strong runnability at very high line speeds. Use in screening: put mono-PP/PCR wipe and wrap structures on the shortlist for wipe-format beauty and personal-care categories.
Source name: Mondi introduces re/loop FlowWrap with 35% post-consumer recycled content for wet wipes packaging — Mondi Group — https://www.mondigroup.com/news-and-insight/2026/mondi-introduces-reloop-flowwrap-with-35-percent-post-consumer-recycled-content-for-wet-wipes-packaging/

Summary of what was found: BOBST’s oneBARRIER PrimeCycle is a transparent AlOx-based full-PE mono-material substrate with a recyclability certification level of 98%; BOBST says it was developed to deliver barrier performance equivalent to non-recyclable mixed-material packaging, especially metallized polyester structures.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: high-barrier recyclable mono-PE film using AlOx barrier technology. Packaging format relevance: high-barrier pouches and flexible structures where conventional mixed-material laminates would otherwise be used. Weight/material reduction relevance: not a direct rigid-vs-flex comparison, but relevant because barrier-rich pouches need not default to heavier or non-recyclable mixed-material structures. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: especially relevant for fragranced, oxygen-sensitive, or moisture/light-sensitive beauty refills that exceed basic mono-PE capability. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe or sensitive-formula conditional shortlist first; likely cost/complexity above entry-level mass-market films. Performance/barrier/recyclability limitation notes: positioned as alternative to metallized polyester-type structures, indicating strong barrier capability with mono-PE outcome. Use in screening: include advanced coated mono-PE barrier solutions in the premium/sensitive-formula shortlist, but screen after basic mono-PE/mono-PP routes on cost and availability.

Summary of what was found: BOBST’s FibreCycle paper-based barrier solution shows why paper-flex routes are conditional rather than default: paper is hygroscopic, rough, brittle, and inherently poor in barrier, so the structure needs primer coating, metallization, and a heat-sealable topcoat to achieve high barrier.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: barrier-coated/metallized paper-fibre flexible packaging. Packaging format relevance: paper-based high-barrier flexible packs intended as alternatives to plastic-based flexible packaging. Weight/material reduction relevance: useful as lightweight and high-paper-share route in some cases, but most valuable because it explains the technical burden required to make fibre-based flex perform. Cosmetic/personal care transfer relevance: potentially relevant to dry or lower-demand refill applications; much less suitable as default route for wet, oily, alcohol-rich, or highly fragranced formulas. Premium versus mass-market fit: visually/premium interesting, but technically conditional and likely better for niche dry products or secondary refill systems than mainstream liquid cosmetics. Performance/barrier limitation notes: paper’s moisture sensitivity, roughness, brittleness, and poor native barrier must be engineered around. Use in screening: treat barrier-coated paper/fibre flex as conditional or early-caution route, behind mono-PE/PP in the liquid beauty and personal-care lightweighting shortlist.
